content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 27, 1994


Honorable Clair Harmony
Mayor Pro-Tem
City of Diamond Bar
P.O. Box 4153
Diamond Bar, CA  91765

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. A-94-135

Dear Mr. Harmony:

     This is in response to your letter requesting confirmation of
advice provided to you on April 11, 1994, regarding the provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     In our telephone conversation, you asked whether the Act
controlled the production or distribution of your newsletter
connected with your status as Mayor Pro-Tem of the City of Diamond
Bar. The newsletter is produced on a personal computer owned by
your campaign, and is distributed by facsimile machine. You
stated that the costs involved in the production or distribution
of the newsletter in that manner were di minimus. You also stated
that there were no public funds used in connection with the
publication or distribution of the newsletter. Currently, your
newsletter has a circulation of 2,400.

     As I advised, since facsimile machine transmissions result in
tangible items being delivered to a recipient, the resulting
document may be subject to the mass mailing² restrictions of

_______________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2  Section 82041.5 defines a "mass mailing" as over two hundred
substantially similar pieces of mail, but does not include a form
letter or other mail which is sent in response to an unsolicited
request, letter or other inquiry.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-135
Page 2


Section 89001 and Regulation 18901. (Harmony Advice Letter, No.
I-91-254.) However, Section 89001 and Regulation 18901 only apply
to publicly funded mass mailings. Since you do not use any public
funds in connection with the production and distribution of your
newsletter, Section 89001 and Regulation 18901 would not apply.

Moreover, as we discussed, Section 84305 of the Act requires
that mass mailings include sender identification. However, since
your newsletter is not sent, it does not appear to be subject to
the requirements of Section 84305. In any case, you stated that
your name and address appeared on the newsletter in a typesize
greater than 6 point. Consequently, even if Section 84305 did
apply, you would be in compliance.3

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.4

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division


3 Please note, that if there are any costs incurred in connection
with the production or distribution of your newsletter they will
be reportable expenditures. You should contact our technical
assistance division regarding campaign disclosure at (916) 322-
5662.

4 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.question: Whether the Political Reform Act controls the production or distribution of a newsletter connected with the recipient's status as Mayor Pro-Tem of the City of Diamond Bar, where the newsletter is produced on a personal computer owned by the campaign and distributed by facsimile machine with de minimis costs and no public funds used.
sections.conclusion: Since the newsletter is not sent (facsimile transmissions result in tangible items being delivered to a recipient, making the resulting document potentially subject to mass mailing restrictions under Section 89001 and Regulation 18901), and no public funds are used, the publicly funded mass mailing restrictions do not apply. Section 84305's sender identification requirements do not apply to non-sent newsletters; however, the newsletter complies in any case as the sender's name and address appear in typesize greater than 6 point. Any costs incurred in newsletter production or distribution will be reportable expenditures.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the Political Reform Act regulate a Mayor Pro-Tem's newsletter produced and distributed via facsimile using campaign equipment and private funds?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that since no public funds are used in the newsletter's production and distribution, the publicly funded mass mailing restrictions do not apply, and the sender identification requirements of Section 84305 do not apply to non-sent materials. Any costs incurred must be reported as expenditures.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a confirmation letter of prior telephone advice dated April 11, 1994. Contains both question and conclusion elements embedded in letter format rather than formal sections. References prior advice letter (Harmony Advice Letter, No. 1-91-254) suggesting this may be a follow-up. Minor ambiguity in conclusion regarding 'not sent' language (appears to be a typographical or transcription issue).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Political Reform Act controls the production or distribution of a newsletter connected with the recipient's status as Mayor Pro-Tem of the City of Diamond Bar, where the newsletter is produced on a personal computer owned by the campaign and distributed by facsimile machine with de minimis costs and no public funds used.

CONCLUSION: Since the newsletter is not sent (facsimile transmissions result in tangible items being delivered to a recipient, making the resulting document potentially subject to mass mailing restrictions under Section 89001 and Regulation 18901), and no public funds are used, the publicly funded mass mailing restrictions do not apply. Section 84305's sender identification requirements do not apply to non-sent newsletters; however, the newsletter complies in any case as the sender's name and address appear in typesize greater than 6 point. Any costs incurred in newsletter production or distribution will be reportable expenditures.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms prior telephone advice regarding whether California's Political Reform Act controls a Mayor Pro-Tem's newsletter produced on campaign equipment and distributed by facsimile with private funds. The FPPC concludes that publicly funded mass mailing restrictions do not apply and sender identification requirements do not apply to non-sent materials, though any associated costs must be reported.
